sentences.text: Arnold Greenblatt.
G-R-double E-N-B-L-A- double T.
A-R-N-O-L-D.
No.
Yes, Yosef Dovid Ben Yehudah.
January 11, 1929.
68-- 67, I'll be 68 in January.
Debrecen, Hungary.
At home Hungarian and Yiddish.
Outside home, Hungarian.
My father's name was Ignatz Greenblatt.
My mother's name was Sarah Greenblatt.
My father was born in [NON-ENGLISH], a small town in Hungary.
My mother was born in [NON-ENGLISH], which is not too far from that same city, town in Hungary.
No, I couldn't tell you.
I wouldn't know how to spell it.
Satmar.
It's that area called Satmar.
Well, my father was a tailor.
He learned from his father.
He worked with about 35 people as a wholesaler in men's suits.
And just before the war broke out, he came to America.
I was at the age of 12 when he left.
He came-- he came by himself, because my grandmother, my mother's mother, was in America.
And my mother pressured him to-- we should all go to America, you know, being that what was happening in Europe, you know, to get away from it.
And he said, I have America here.
And he decided my mother wants to come so badly, so he left to America.
And then the war broke out.
What I remember about my father that he was a very charitable man, a very religious man.

When it came-- what's that holiday we have-- Hanukkah-- he would come to our school and measure the needy children for suits and coats and things, and he would give them for Hanukkah as-- as free clothing.
A very good father, a very good provider, strict.
Every Friday night he would call me and set me in his lap, and ask me what I learned during the week, in cheder, when I learned Yiddish and Hebrew.
Well, he-- I can't say nuthin' bad about him.
He was a great man.
My mother also, a very charitable person, very religious.
She used to tell my father every Friday night, when we went to shul, to bring somebody home for supper.
You know, [NON-ENGLISH], somebody that had no family in the city.
And he did so.
I remember it quite well.
Pardon me?
Yes, yes.
We had a sleep-in maid, until the Jews were not allowed to employ Gentiles.
Her name in Hungarian was Bushke.
That's, I think, Betty it's called you know, in English.
No.
No, I was a young fellow, a little guy.
Yes, I have one brother and two sisters.
My oldest sister is 73.
Her name is Clara-- her married name?
T-H-A-W, Thaw.
My brother's is-- my brother is Ernie Greenblatt.
He is 70.
He's three years older than I am.
And my younger sister is Judy Stevens, is married name, who is a year younger than myself, which is 67, 66.
Well, my sister is a housewife.
She has three children.
Her husband passed away about five years ago, lives in Florida.
My brother is in-- lives in New Jersey.
He was in a dental business, dental lab.
He's got two children, a boy and a girl.
He's financially very stable.
And he still comes into Brooklyn to work, at the age of 70, from New Jersey.
He was the founder of my synagogue that I belong to, president at one time, charitable man, a good-hearted man, a good father.
My youngest sister who lives in California, who is brokenhearted from what happened to our mother, with what we've been through-- she was a little girl.
And she's a mother of two children, and a grandmother of two.
And she still works.
Before the war, my younger sister, I looked out for her, as the younger sister, from being attacked by the Gentiles comin' out of school.
I protected her.
And we had great love for each other.
We still do.
My older brother, my brother, he-- he was like a father to me when my dad left for America.
Because I was the type of kid that didn't want to go to cheder.
And when the anti-Semitism started, I cut off my payos, and I let my hair grow.
And before that, I was more of-- of the Orthodox style.
And he beat me up once in while [LAUGHS] as-- you know, as punishment for not going to cheder.
But I still love him very dearly.
In Debrecen.
Yes, Debrecen is a fairly large city.
It has trolley cars.
It had public swimming pool in the summertime, for recreation, Olympic-sized pools out in the forest.
It had great hospitals.
Because I was in one of them, and they was going to operate on me, and I ran away.
So I-- they thought that I had an appendix problem.
And I still have my appendix, by the way.
It was never taken out.
I ran away.
It has a large hotel, which I've been to recently.
As a child, I used to admire people sitting outside reading the paper in front of the hotel.
There was like an enclosure, so that to keep the public from interfering.
And people were drinking beer or coffee.
It's an interesting city, very nice.
It was, anyway.
As of now, I wouldn't want to live there anymore.
But it's a nice city.
Orthodox.
I went to a rabbi.
In those days we had rabbis that had their own school in their house, where they had 15-20 children.
And then I had a private tutor.
When I got caught not going to the-- to the rabbi.
And my mother hired a private tutor to teach me [NON-ENGLISH].
Falkowicz was one.
As a matter of fact, he was a very tough rabbi.
He used a very, very straight stick.
If you missed-- the other one, I don't remember.
No.
The shul I went to was the large orthodox shul, with a balcony on both-- or all around except the front, where the [NON-ENGLISH] was.
But the women were upstairs, the men downstairs with the [NON-ENGLISH] in the center.
They had about 35 [NON-ENGLISH], and when it came Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, I sang in a choir, I sang solo.
I-- with our cantor who unfortunately was massacred by the Germans.
His name was Fishl Silber, the best chazzan that I ever heard in my life, up until today.
[NON-ENGLISH], and nobody could go up against him.
That's how great he was.
We used to practice before yontif.
My favorite yontif was [NON-ENGLISH], where with the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, they're going around with a Torah, with a procession that never ended.
Shul was-- our shul was decorated with flowers that when you walked in, you thought you were in a garden somewhere.
It was so beautiful.
That was the year that was the shul that was.
That was beautiful.
I would say 500.
Including the women.
Yes.
He was a very, very old rabbi, with a long white beard.
His name was Strasser.
I-- I wouldn't know how to spell his name.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A distinguished rabbi, and then there was the shamas, the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, that was all part of their employees of the shul.
No.
The only one I remember is the cantor, and the-- oh yes, yeah, the [NON-ENGLISH] Gross, the Yiddish name, I don't know.
Because he has a son here that's a chazzan in the Forest Hills Jewish Center, Yeah, his name was Gross.
Yes.
Typical Shabbos, yes.
Typical Shabbos was, going way back when I was very young, my mother sent us to the mikvah.
We came home, got dressed.
And Friday night she light candles, and my father and my brother and I went to shul.
In the early years, my younger years where we didn't have any hatred from the goyim, it was very pleasant.
Shabbos, we went to shul.
On the way home, we stopped at the bakery, picked up the [NON-ENGLISH].
The neighborhood smelled from when the baker opened the oven.
All the children were coming out-- they could smell it all over the neighborhood.
It was so nice.
My mother baked challahs.
No.
Well, before you're 13, you-- you carry it.
There was no eruv no.
Because as a child I remember my cousin was born.
And his bris was on Shabbos.
And I had to carry the knife for the the mohel.
You know, these things I remember like it was yesterday.
And then, of course, they had the kiddush in the house.
Shabbos was very nice.
Friends come over, and they play.
And as a matter of fact we had a little hallway like where I had my own little shul as-- as a child.
I-- I was a very good Jew that time.
I still am, you know.
But it was-- it was a nice life, until the Nazis got stronger, and when we come out of shul, we used to got into fights, you know.
They used to attack us.
They would beat you, you know, take sticks or stones, they throw at you.
[BACKROUND NOISE] That was when the Nazis came in.
Then the Hungarians, of course, sided with them, you know, the Gentiles.
That happened before, yes, the fascists, yes.
Less-- as an individual level?
Yes, there was.
Fights, you know, dirty Jew.
If you had a payos, they pulled on it.
I personally was stuck into a pile of snow head first.
I was a-- I was a fighter.
I never turned the other cheek, or run away from a good fight.
I was a big boy for my age, and-- and very nervy, and I fought back, and especially if it had to do to protect my sister.
You know, when she came out of school or she would be attacked, I was right there to protect her.
Pesach, I remember very well.
I was-- before Pesach I was bitten by a dog.
It was-- the matzah factory was in a basement of our school.
And in Europe, matzah was a novelty, you know, when Pesach came.
So we would, kids come out of school, we would go to the basement window and ask, can I have a piece of matzah?
And this dog was roaming around, and somehow I kicked it or chased it.
And the dog chased me, and caught me, and bit me.
[LAUGHS] Pesach was a nice, nice yontif.
My father had Seder, sit on a pillow, put on a kettle.
As for that matter, not only Pesach, but every-- every Friday night, or Shabbos after a meal, we sat at the table, the table everybody together, and we sang [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, of course, I was asked to [NON-ENGLISH], because I was the younger.
And it was something to look forward to.
It was nice.
[NON-ENGLISH], for one, for Shabbos.
In the summertime, we used to have cold food, fruit soup, watermelons, summer fruits and things like that.
And of course, the beautiful challahs she baked, cakes, kindl which was-- still my favorite.
That if I see it, I can't turn away from it.
And the maid used to go to [NON-ENGLISH] to check the-- the-- what do they call the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, for the-- for the ducks, to see if it's kosher, you know, if it's good.
These are things-- I mean there's many things that-- that comes to me throughout the years.
When I have my own Seder here with my children and grandchildren it brings back memories.
Let's see.
No.
Well, basically the shul that I belong to is about the same.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's sukkah, where I always build the sukkah for my father.
That was an exciting thing.
No, not really.
By numbers, I wouldn't know.
The Gentiles was a larger percentage, more-- more goyim than Jews.
I would say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 70 and 30.
Well, I had some good ones.
I had non-Jewish friends that lived on the same street.
As a matter of fact, they offered to hide me.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't forget, it's over 50 years ago.
So I don't remember their names.
But I would not leave my mother and my sister and brother.
Without me they would have never survived.
And the relationship, in general, wasn't too bad, until the Germans came in and they tried to-- try to get what the Jews had.
There were some good in 'em.
Yes.
There was some good.
But there was a lot of them not good, bad.
There was no particular name to the school.
The school that I attended to learn Hungarian, you know, the Hungarian school, it belonged to the shul, to the synagogue.
There was one particular block, where half of the block, the building was a four-story building.
It was a Jewish school.
And the the other school was a big wall separated it-- was the public school, the goyim, Gentile.
So there was no-- really no name.
It was the orthodox school, that's all.
That's all as far as I can remember.
What subjects?
History, math, normal what the things that you learn in school.
Cheder [NON-ENGLISH], Rashi, Gemara, of course I had kindergarten, pre-kindergarten, to learn how to daven.
Don't ask me today whether I know it.
I know how to daven.
That's about it.
[LAUGHS]
Oh yes, lots of friends.
As a young person, as a very young I wanted to be a chazzan.
Yes, I sang in the choir.
And then I got interested in bicycles, motorcycles, sewing machines.
You know, my father had 30 of them.
And I used to clean them and fix them as a young boy.
I liked to work.
And I'm still doing it.
I still like to work.
It's just my hobby, workin'.
I like to create.
I like challenges.
I like to build, and then look at it, that I did it.
I accomplished it.
And I'm proud of it.
Just like I'm proud of my family.
I couldn't believe it, that it could happen.
I mean, we had such a good life.
I heard about it, as a kid of course, in Lithuania and Poland it started much earlier than it did in-- in Hungary.
And when we heard that the Jews are being deported, and the train was coming through Debrecen, through the city, I volunteered with other young boys.
We took a basket, and we collected food from the people.
And we met the train to give it to the people.
And I could never imagine that that would happen in a city-- in a country where I was born, where we lived.
Because life was so beautiful.
And those days, my father was home yet.
But it happened.
When the Nazis, well, I heard it in shul, most of the things you hear, you know, like they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH], you know, I heard it being said.
We used to listen to the Voice of America.
When it came a time when Jews were not allowed to have radios, like you couldn't employ any Gentiles.
And I used to listen to it in a basement, the Voice of America.
Yes, you know, in secret.
Like I would-- they would send me out in a yard to make sure nobody is coming, you know, nobody comes in.
And that's how we got-- we got the news what was going on.
And until one day I remember we were planning our Purim play in school.
And the Germans raided Debrecen, you know, the city.
Yeah, like the day before, yeah.
And then after that things just went bad.
Yes.
Well, only what we saw on the street, you know, the-- the Hungarian newspaper.
No.
Some, not not all of it, you know.
About the Jews, of course, they boasted that they were deporting Jews, and Jews here, the Jews there.
Jews can't do this, and you know, certain things.
But very little, not-- not much.
OK.
